turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some issues with my stomach lately. I get this crampy pain in my lower abdomen, mostly on the left side, and I've also been going to the bathroom a lot more than usual – about 8-10 times a day, with loose stools that are pretty urgent.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's really frustrating because this pain is constant and it's not just the cramps, it's also this burning sensation that I get after eating. And the diarrhea is getting worse - it's affecting my work performance, I'm tired all the time, and I feel like I have no energy to do anything except go to the bathroom.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: I get really fatigued during an episode, it's like my whole body just feels exhausted. And I've noticed that sometimes I feel a little dizzy or lightheaded after using the bathroom, especially if I haven't eaten much in a while.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: To be honest, I don't really notice any significant sweating or clamminess during an episode. But to be clear, the dizziness and lightheadedness I mentioned earlier can sometimes make me feel a bit sweaty on my forehead, but it's not like I'm sweating profusely or anything.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No significant perspiration, just some occasional sweat on my forehead when I'm feeling dizzy or lightheaded.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: It pretty much stays on the left side of my lower abdomen, but sometimes it can radiate up towards my back a bit. The cramping and burning sensation seem to be centered around that area, mostly on the left side, though.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: No, it doesn't radiate to my arms, neck, or jaw. The pain is pretty localized to my lower abdomen, mostly on the left side, with some occasional backache when I'm having a bad episode.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I don't experience any tingling or numbness in my arms during an episode.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, nothing like that. It's all centered around my lower abdomen, mostly on the left side, with some backache occasionally when I'm having a bad episode, but no tingling, numbness, or pain in my arms or legs.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: Actually, I have been noticing some achy pain in both knees and ankles lately. There's no swelling or redness, it's just a general ache that comes and goes, but it's been pretty consistent over the past few weeks.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: I'm not sure about my shoes feeling tighter, I don't think so. And no, there's no puffiness in my legs either. Like I said, it's just this general achy pain in my knees and ankles that's been bothering me lately, but nothing like swelling or redness.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really felt nauseous or queasy during these episodes. The cramping and burning sensation can make me feel a bit uncomfortable after eating, but it's not like I've had any actual nausea or vomiting. But like I said earlier, I do get this urgent need to use the bathroom right after eating, which is really frustrating.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Honestly, no, I don't usually feel nauseous or queasy during these episodes. The discomfort and pain are more crampy and burning in nature, rather than a feeling of sickness to my stomach.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: Yes, I've been experiencing low-grade fever, not too high, but just a slight increase in temperature, usually around 37.8-38.0°C, and it happens a few times per week.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: Actually, I have lost about 4 kg (9 lbs) over the past 3 weeks, which is really concerning for me. My appetite hasn't been that great lately due to the constant discomfort and urgency of using the bathroom, but I don't think that's the main reason behind the weight loss.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient:
To be honest, my diet hasn't been great lately. With my long work hours, I've been mostly eating takeout or ordering food online because it's just easier and faster. Sometimes I'll grab something quick from a nearby restaurant or convenience store. I don't really have time to cook for myself or prepare healthy meals.

As for exercise, I try to go for a run or do some light stretching at home when I can, but lately, my fatigue has been making it tough to maintain any kind of regular routine. I used to be pretty active and enjoyed going for runs, but now even short walks feel like they're exhausting me.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: To be honest, my physical activity has been really limited lately. I used to enjoy running and working out, but with this constant fatigue and discomfort, even short walks feel like a chore. I try to do some light stretching at home when I can, but that's about it. My work hours are pretty long, so finding time for regular exercise is tough.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my diet has been pretty irregular lately due to my long work hours and busy schedule. I usually end up grabbing something quick from a restaurant or ordering takeout online because it's just easier and faster. Sometimes I'll have a sandwich or leftovers for lunch if I can scrounge them up from the fridge, but dinner is often a mix of fast food, pizza, or Chinese takeout.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's been a concern for me because I'm getting similar symptoms, and I'm worried it might be something like that too.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: Yes, my older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's been a concern for me because I'm experiencing similar symptoms and I'm worried it might be something like that too.
